seoTitle: MPS Technical Support | Power Management Application Notes | BeiLuo
seoDescription: Technical support resources for MPS power management ICs including application notes, selection guides, design calculators, and troubleshooting assistance.

faqs.question: Where can I find MPS product documentation?
faqs.answer: MPS product datasheets, application notes, and reference designs are available on our website. BeiLuo provides localized technical support and can assist with specific application questions. Contact our FAE team for additional resources.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our technical resources section or contact support for specific documentation requests.

faqs.question: How do I get technical support for my MPS design?
faqs.answer: BeiLuo provides comprehensive technical support for MPS products including schematic review, layout guidance, and troubleshooting. Our FAE team has extensive experience with MPS power management solutions. Contact us through the support portal, email, or phone for assistance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Submit a support request with your design details for personalized technical assistance.

articles.title: DC-DC Converter Selection Guide
articles.description: Comprehensive guide to selecting the right MPS DC-DC converter for your application, covering topology selection, component sizing, and design trade-offs.
articles.summary: This guide covers DC-DC converter selection criteria including input/output requirements, efficiency targets, size constraints, and feature needs. Learn how to choose between buck, boost, and buck-boost topologies.
articles.content: Selecting the right DC-DC converter is critical for power supply performance and reliability. This guide covers key selection criteria and design considerations for MPS DC-DC converters. Buck converters step down voltage and are the most common topology. They offer high efficiency and simple design. Use when output voltage is lower than input. Boost converters step up voltage for applications where output must be higher than input. Buck-boost converters handle both step-up and step-down when input voltage can be above or below output. Key selection parameters include input voltage range, output voltage and current, efficiency requirements, size constraints, and feature requirements. MPS offers comprehensive DC-DC solutions from micro-power to high-current modules.

faq.question: How do I calculate inductor value for a buck converter?
faq.answer: Inductor value L = (Vout × (Vin - Vout)) / (Vin × Fsw × ΔI). Where ΔI is inductor ripple current, typically 20-40% of output current. Higher inductance reduces ripple but increases size. Lower inductance allows smaller size but higher ripple. For a 12V to 5V conversion at 500kHz with 3A output and 30% ripple: L = (5 × (12-5)) / (12 × 500000 × 0.9) = 6.5µH. Standard value 6.8µH would be appropriate.
faq.decisionGuide: Use MPS inductor selection tools or contact our FAE team for specific recommendations.

faq.question: What is the difference between COT and voltage mode control?
faq.answer: Constant On-Time (COT) control provides fast transient response by varying off-time while keeping on-time constant. Benefits include simple compensation, fast transient response, and good light-load efficiency. Voltage mode control uses fixed frequency with PWM duty cycle modulation. Benefits include predictable switching frequency and easier synchronization. MPS COT converters like MPQ8862 offer excellent transient response for applications with pulsed loads.
faq.decisionGuide: Choose COT for fast transient requirements. Choose voltage mode for fixed frequency needs.

faq.question: How do I select output capacitors?
faq.answer: Output capacitor selection depends on ripple voltage and transient requirements. For ripple: Cout_min = ΔI / (8 × Fsw × ΔV_ripple). For transient: Cout_min = (ΔI_step × t_response) / ΔV_transient. Use ceramic capacitors for low ESR and small size. Add electrolytic capacitors for bulk capacitance if needed. Consider DC bias derating for ceramic caps. MPS provides capacitor selection guidelines in datasheets.
faq.decisionGuide: Use MPS calculation tools or contact us for capacitor selection assistance.

faeInsights.summary: The most common mistake in DC-DC design is underestimating inductor ripple current and output capacitor requirements. Always calculate worst-case conditions and include margin for component tolerances.
keyTakeaways: Size output caps for transient requirements
Consider DC bias derating for ceramics

customerCases.feedback: Following the selection guide helped us choose the optimal MPS converter, reducing power dissipation by 20% compared to our previous design.

articles.title: Power Supply PCB Layout Best Practices
articles.description: Essential PCB layout guidelines for switching power supplies to ensure stable operation, minimize EMI, and optimize thermal performance.
articles.summary: Learn critical layout techniques for switching power supplies including component placement, trace routing, ground plane design, and thermal management for reliable operation.
articles.content: Proper PCB layout is essential for switching power supply performance. Poor layout can cause instability, excessive EMI, and thermal issues. This guide covers best practices for power supply layout. Minimize switching loop area by placing input capacitors close to VIN and PGND pins. This reduces parasitic inductance and EMI. Use wide, short traces for high-current paths. Multiple vias help distribute current and reduce resistance. Implement solid ground planes with minimal splits. Connect analog ground and power ground at a single point near the IC. Place feedback divider close to FB pin with short traces. Keep sensitive analog traces away from switching nodes. Use thermal vias under power ICs to transfer heat to inner layers. Provide adequate copper area for heat spreading.

faq.question: How do I minimize switching loop area?
faq.answer: Minimize the loop formed by input capacitor, high-side MOSFET, and low-side MOSFET (or diode). Place input capacitors within 2-3mm of VIN and PGND pins. Use wide copper pours for these connections. The switching node (SW) should be wide but as short as possible. Avoid vias in the switching loop as they add inductance. For buck converters, the critical loop is: Cin+ → HS FET → LS FET → Cin-. Keep this loop area under 25mm² for best EMI performance.
faq.decisionGuide: Follow MPS reference layouts or request layout review services for critical designs.

faq.question: What ground plane configuration is recommended?
faq.answer: Use a solid ground plane on an internal layer with minimal splits. Connect PGND (power ground) and AGND (analog ground) at a single point near the IC, typically under the thermal pad. This prevents switching noise from coupling into sensitive analog circuits. Use multiple vias (9-16) in the thermal pad to connect to inner ground planes. Keep high-current return paths separate from sensitive signal returns until the single-point connection.
faq.decisionGuide: Use 4-layer boards with dedicated ground plane for best performance. Contact us for layer stackup recommendations.

faq.question: How do I implement thermal vias properly?
faq.answer: Thermal vias transfer heat from the IC thermal pad to inner copper layers. Use 0.3mm diameter vias with 1.0mm pitch (9-16 vias typical). Fill or tent vias to prevent solder wicking during assembly. Place vias directly under the IC thermal pad. Connect to solid copper pours on inner layers for heat spreading. For high-power applications, use thermal vias to backside copper with exposed metal for heatsink attachment.
faq.decisionGuide: Use MPS thermal simulation tools to optimize via count and placement for your specific design.

faeInsights.summary: Layout is often the difference between a working power supply and a problematic one. Invest time in proper component placement and minimize switching loop area. The extra effort pays off in stable operation and reduced EMI.

customerCases.feedback: Implementing proper layout guidelines eliminated the EMI issues we were experiencing and improved efficiency by 3%.

articles.title: Power Supply Thermal Design Guide
articles.description: Comprehensive thermal design guidelines for power management ICs including thermal calculations, PCB design, and heatsink selection.
articles.summary: Learn thermal design fundamentals for power supplies including junction temperature calculations, thermal resistance management, and cooling strategies for reliable operation.
articles.content: Thermal design is critical for power supply reliability and performance. Excessive temperature reduces efficiency, accelerates aging, and can cause failure. This guide covers thermal design fundamentals for MPS power management ICs. Junction temperature Tj = Ta + Pd × Rth-ja, where Ta is ambient, Pd is power dissipation, and Rth-ja is junction-to-ambient thermal resistance. Keep Tj below maximum rating (typically 125°C or 150°C) with margin. Thermal resistance has three components: junction-to-case (Rth-jc), case-to-heatsink (Rth-ch), and heatsink-to-ambient (Rth-ha). For PCB-mounted ICs without heatsinks, Rth-ja depends on PCB copper area, thickness, and airflow. Increasing copper area reduces thermal resistance. Typical values range from 30°C/W for large copper area to 80°C/W for minimal copper.

faq.question: How do I calculate maximum allowed power dissipation?
faq.answer: Pd_max = (Tj_max - Ta) / Rth-ja. For example, with Tj_max = 125°C, Ta = 60°C, and Rth-ja = 40°C/W: Pd_max = (125 - 60) / 40 = 1.625W. If your actual dissipation exceeds this, you need to reduce thermal resistance (more copper, heatsink, airflow) or reduce power. Always include margin - design for Tj 20-30°C below maximum for reliability.
faq.decisionGuide: Use MPS thermal calculators to determine maximum power for your specific conditions.

faq.question: What copper area do I need for my design?
faq.answer: Copper area depends on power dissipation and ambient temperature. General guidelines: 1W dissipation needs 5-10cm² copper. 2W needs 15-25cm². 3W+ needs heatsink or forced airflow. Use 2oz copper for better heat spreading. Multiple layers with thermal vias improve performance. For high power, consider exposed metal on backside with heatsink. MPS datasheets provide thermal curves for different copper areas.
faq.decisionGuide: Refer to MPS thermal performance curves or contact us for thermal design assistance.

faq.question: When do I need a heatsink?
faq.answer: Heatsinks are needed when PCB copper area alone cannot maintain safe junction temperature. Consider heatsinks when: 1) Power dissipation exceeds 2-3W. 2) Ambient temperature is high (>60°C). 3) Space constraints limit copper area. 4) Reliability requirements demand lower operating temperature. Heatsink selection depends on thermal resistance requirements, airflow, and mechanical constraints. Thermal interface material (TIM) is required between IC and heatsink.
faq.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for heatsink selection and thermal simulation services.

faeInsights.summary: Thermal issues are a leading cause of field failures. Always calculate worst-case junction temperature and include margin. When in doubt, provide more copper area or add a heatsink. The cost of over-designing thermals is much less than a field failure.
keyTakeaways: Use adequate copper area or heatsinks

customerCases.feedback: Following the thermal design guide helped us achieve reliable operation at 85°C ambient with proper copper area and thermal vias.
